coredata.dc:title: Hemin-incorporated nanoflowers as enzyme mimics for colorimetric detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Kui-Yu
Bu, Sheng-Jun
Ju, Chuan-Jing
Li, Chang-Tian
Li, Zhong-Yi
Han, Ye
Ma, Cheng-You
Wang, Cheng-Yu
Hao, Zhuo
Liu, Wen-Sen
Wan, Jia-Yu
coredata.dc:description: Rapid, sensitive and point-of-care detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria is essential for food safety. In this study, we found that hemin-concanavalin A hybrid nanoflowers (HCH nanoflowers), as solid mimic peroxidase, could catalyze oxidation of 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) in the presence of H2O2 to a green-colored product. HCH nanoflowers, integrating the essential functions of both biological recognition and signal amplification, meet the requirements of signal labels for colorimetric immunoassay of bacteria. In view of the excellent peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of HCH nanoflowers, a colorimetric biosensing platform was newly constructed and applied for sensitive detection of foodborne Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7). The corresponding detection limits was as low as 4.1 CFU/mL with wide linear ranges (101–106 CFU/mL).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid nanoflowers
Enzyme mimics
Colorimetric detection
Pathogenic bacteria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric analyses of peroxidase-like catalytic activities of HCH nanoflowers
Kinetics study of HCH nanoflowers as peroxidase mimics
Principle of the proposed colorimetric immunoassay biosensor
Optimization of detection conditions for colorimetric immunoassay
Performance of colorimetric immunoassay for E. coli O157:H7 detection
Detection of E. coli O157:H7 in milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical absorbance of different solutions. (A) a. HCH nanoflowers; b. ABTS + H2O2; c. Con A hybrid nanoflowers + ABTS + H2O2; d. HCH nanoflowers + ABTS + H2O2. (B) Photographs of the color-generating reaction of ABTS, TMB, and OPD with HCH nanoflowers in the presence of H2O2.
Steady-state kinetic assay of HCH nanoflowers: (A) 4 mM ABTS with different amounts of H2O2. (B) 50 mM H2O2 with different amounts of ABTS. (C, D) Double-reciprocal plots of (A, B), respectively.

(A) Digital photographs, (B) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (C) the linear relationship between the absorption intensity changes at 414 nm and the log of concentrations of target E. coli O157:H7 from 10 to 107 CFU/mL.

Selectivity of the proposed method determined using different bacteria (the concentration of each pathogenic bacterial sample was 1 × 103 CFU/mL). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.
Schematic illustration of the colorimetric immunoassay based on HCH nanoflowers.
Recoveries of E. coli O157:H7 spiked real samples.

row.entry: E. coli O157:H7 found (mean ± RSD, CFU/mL)

head.ce:title: Hemin-incorporated nanoflowers as enzyme mimics for colorimetric detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equal work to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid, sensitive and point-of-care detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria is essential for food safety. In this study, we found that hemin-concanavalin A hybrid nanoflowers (HCH nanoflowers), as solid mimic peroxidase, could catalyze oxidation of 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) in the presence of H2O2 to a green-colored product. HCH nanoflowers, integrating the essential functions of both biological recognition and signal amplification, meet the requirements of signal labels for colorimetric immunoassay of bacteria. In view of the excellent peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of HCH nanoflowers, a colorimetric biosensing platform was newly constructed and applied for sensitive detection of foodborne Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7). The corresponding detection limits was as low as 4.1 CFU/mL with wide linear ranges (101–106 CFU/mL).

ce:sections.ce:para: Sensitive and specific determination of foodborne pathogenic bacteria has been attracting significant attention in food quality control 1,2 , and a huge effort has been taken to develop point-of-care assay. Based on different signal-generation principles, colorimetric immunoassays can be employed for this purpose because of advantages such as visual observation with naked eye and simple operations. 3,4 Owing to its high catalytic efficiency and rapid analysis under mild conditions, heme protein-horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is usually used as an amplifying label in colorimetric immunoassays to catalyze the colorless peroxidase substrate to yield colorimetric output signal. 5 However, as a natural enzyme, HRP has some intrinsic disadvantages, such as low stability and less adaptability to harsh environment. 6 In addition, high cost and time-consuming preparation and purification process also limit practical application of HRP. Therefore, it is important and remains a challenge to develop novel and efficient artificial peroxidase mimics that can overcome certain drawbacks of natural enzymes.

With the rapid development of nanotechnology, artificial enzymes possessing peroxidase-like activity have emerged as a new class of ideal and tools for the colorimetric detection. When compared with HRP, peroxidase mimics have some advantages such as low cost, facile synthesis, and higher chemical stability. Since the first report of the inherent peroxidase-like activity of magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles, similar to that of HRP, in 2007 7 , some inorganic materials, such as gold nanomaterials 8 , silver nanoparticles 9 , quantum dots 10 , carbon composite 11 , and metal-organic frameworks 12 , have been developed as innovative and powerful enzyme mimetics with peroxidase-like activity. After controlling and tailoring their properties in a very predictable manner, these enzyme-like nanomaterials have been widely used in a variety of fields, including biosensing, immunoassays, cancer diagnostics and therapy 13 , environmental pollutant adsorbent 14 , etc. Nevertheless, lower efficiency of nanozymes has created a rather large gap between nanozymes and protein-based enzymes 15 , and further effort to find or develop superior artificial enzymes with higher efficiency is still a research hotspot. 16

Hemin (iron porphyrin derivative), the active center of heme proteins family, including peroxidases, myoglobins, hemoglobins, and cytochromes, has attracted widespread attention because of its mimic peroxidase activity similar to that of the peroxidase enzyme. 17 It is easily available and cost-effective, and has been used as peroxidase mimetics. 18,19 However, the catalytic activity of hemin is easily hindered because of its oxidative self-degradation and molecular aggregation to inactive dimers in liquid solution. 20 Nevertheless, free hemin, possessing weaker mimicking peroxidase activity, when compared with HRP, cannot be integrated into traditional immunoassay platform. 21,22 To solve these problems, hemin has been immobilized on some high surface area nanomaterials, e.g., graphene, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), porous carbon, metal/metal oxide nanostructures, and carbon nanotubes, which improved its stability and catalytic activity similar to those of natural peroxidases, leading to its successful use as peroxidase mimic for extensive applications.

Since the effective preparation of protein-organic flower-like nanostructure by Zare et al. (2012) in, hybrid nanoflowers have attracted substantial attention because of their high surface area-to-volume ratios and one-pot green preparation. 23,24 Hybrid nanoflowers have been exploited as a potential matrix for biomolecule immobilization, and have been found to exhibit higher activity and excellent stability than their free counterparts. 25–27 Moreover, HRP, as an organic component, has been prepared in the form of biomolecule-inorganic crystal hybrid nanoflowers, and low-level H2O2 and phenol have been respectively analyzed successfully by using as-prepared HRP-inorganic nanoflowers. Furthermore, antibody-HRP inorganic hybrid nanoflowers have been employed as a novel enzyme-labeled antibody of sandwich immunoassay for the detection of pathogenic bacteria.

However, to the best of our knowledge, hemin-incorporated hybrid nanoflowers have not yet been developed. Therefore, in the present study, we synthesized novel multifunctional peroxidase mimic hybrid nanoflowers by encapsulating hemin, and the resultant labels were found to be ideal for sandwich-type colorimetric detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria. To the best of our knowledge, hemin-concanavalin A (Con A) hybrid nanoflowers (HCH nanoflowers) is the first time to use for the colorimetric detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria. The one-pot green process of hemin-nanoflowers only involved simple mixing of Con A and hemin in PBS solution containing copper ions at room temperature. The HCH nanoflowers were observed to possess peroxidase-like activity with the ability to catalyze ABTS to generate a green chromogenic product in the presence of H2O2. As Con A has high binding efficiency with O-antigen of foodborne E. coli O157:H7 surface, HCH nanoflowers could selectively recognize E. coli O157:H7. 28,29 The regular morphology and uniform size of HCH nanoflowers ensured that the equal amount of HCH nanoflowers labels were connected to each bacterial cell, leading to good linearity in E. coli O157:H7 detection. The combination of the nanoflowers-catalyzed chromogenic reaction and magnetic separation resulted in a simple specific and sensitive colorimetric approach for E. coli O157:H7 detection. The linear range for E. coli O157:H7 detection was noted to be from 101 to 106 CFU/mL with a detection limit of 4.1 CFU/mL. The assay has the potential for point-of-care detecting various foodborne pathogenic bacteria for food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: The general procedure for the synthesis of HCH nanoflowers is shown in Fig. S1. Briefly, the nanoflowers were prepared by incorporating hemin and Con A in a PBS solution containing copper (II) sulfate at 25 °C. After 24 h, a blue precipitate with porous, flower-like structures was obtained. The morphology of the HCH nanoflowers was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. The SEM image of the HCH nanoflowers revealed hierarchical structures with high surface-to-volume ratios (Fig. S1A and B). TEM image indicated that the as-prepared HCH nanoflowers were mostly spherical in shape with an average size of 5 μm (Fig. S1C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric analyses of peroxidase-like catalytic activities of HCH nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the catalytic oxidation abilities of HCH nanoflowers, the representative peroxidase substrate ABTS was chosen as the chromogenic substrate. The HCH nanoflowers could catalyze the oxidation of ABTS in the presence of H2O2 to produce an obvious green color product, with an optimum absorption peak centered at 414 nm (curve d in Fig. 1 A). By compare, the control experiments performed in the absence of HCH nanoflowers or H2O2 showed little oxidative reaction and did not yield an absorption band at 414 nm (curves a and b in Fig. 1A). These results indicated that the reaction of ABTS with H2O2 was significantly expedited by HCH nanoflowers. As shown in Fig. 1A (curve c), under identical reaction conditions, Con A hybrid nanoflowers could not display similar peroxidase-like activity to yield an absorption band at 414 nm, indicating that the observed catalysis behaviors originated from HCH nanoflowers. To further describe the peroxidase-like activities of HCH nanoflowers, we also investigated the reaction of HCH nanoflowers with other typical peroxidase substrates, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and o-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride (OPD) (Fig. 1B). The results obtained confirmed that HCH nanoflowers are efficient peroxidase mimics imitating HRP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics study of HCH nanoflowers as peroxidase mimics
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the enzyme kinetics methods and theory, the catalytic performance of HCH nanoflowers with ABTS and H2O2 as chromogenic substrates was further studied for acquiring the steady-state kinetic parameters (Fig. 2 A and B). Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) was obtained by using Lineweaver-Burk plots (Fig. 2C and D). The Km value for the HCH nanoflowers with ABTS as the substrate was 0.2248 mM, which is obviously lower than those of HRP 30,31 (0.81 mM, 2 mM), NiFe2O4 MNPs (0.46 mM) 32 , and WOx QDs (0.79 mM) 10 , suggesting that HCH nanoflowers exhibited higher affinity to ABTS than natural enzymes. In contrast, the Km value of HCH nanoflowers with H2O2 as the substrate was 7.665 mM, which is higher than HRP, indicating that a higher H2O2 concentration is required to achieve maximum activity of HCH nanoflowers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the proposed colorimetric immunoassay biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the new colorimetric immunoassay proposed in this study for sensitive and selective detection of E. coli O157:H7 using HCH nanoflowers colorimetric conversion strategy. By using a representative sandwich-type immunoassay as a model system, magnetic beads labeled polyclonal antibody and HCH nanoflowers are used as the capture antibody and detection antibody, respectively. In the presence of target bacteria, the HCH nanoflowers and polyclonal antibody modified MMs couple with the surface of E. coli O157:H7. After the immunoassay reaction, the HCH nanoflowers captured in the sandwich-type immunosensing system react with ABTS to generate a green product in the presence of H2O2. Through this novel nanoflowers colorimetric detection process, a highly visible color change is achieved which can be observed with naked eye. In the absence of E. coli O157:H7, the HCH nanoflowers can’t be captured from the supernatant because of the lack of specific recognition. As a result, the E. coli O157:H7 can be specifically detected by measuring the separated HCH nanoflowers catalyzing chromogenic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for colorimetric immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain an optimal performance of the colorimetric immunoassay, the parameters, including Con A and hemin feeding mass ratio in hybrid nanoflowers, pH, temperature, and reaction time, which may influence the sensitivity of the proposed method, were optimized. The catalytic reaction was detected by recording the change in ABTS absorbance at 414 nm. The optical densities of the experimental group (1.0 × 103 CFU/mL E. coli O157:H7) and control group (0 CFU/mL E. coli O157:H7) were examined at the same time for assessing the impacts of a variety of factors on sensitivity of the proposed method.

First, the effect of Con A and hemin feeding mass ratio was investigated. The colorimetric signal was regulated by the amount of immobilized hemin in the hybrid nanoflowers. The recognition efficiency between HCH nanoflowers and target bacteria was influenced by loading amount of immobilized Con A. Hence, it can be concluded that Con A and hemin feeding mass ratios played a large role in colorimetric detection of E. coli O157:H7. The optimum colorimetric signal was acquired with the Con A and hemin feeding mass ratio of 2:1 (total organic components, 0.09 mg/mL; Fig. S2A). The effects of pH, reaction temperature and incubation time were also researched. As shown in Fig. S2B–D, the highest colorimetric signal was obtained at pH 6.5 and 25 °C. The structure of the hybrid nanoflowers easily gets broken under extreme pH conditions 33. Under acidic or alkaline conditions, the activity of antibody and protein is easily lost. At room temperature of 25 °C, it can promote the function of antibody and protein activity and facilitate the experiment operation. As a result, 25 °C and pH 6.5 were chosen as the optimal reaction temperature and pH for the reaction, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2D, the signals absorbance enhanced with the increasing incubation time and a balance was reached when the incubation time was 90 min. The incubation time of nanoflowers also can influence the capture efficiency between HCH nanoflowers and target bacteria. When the incubation time of nanoflowers is not enough, the capture efficiency is low, resulting in low signal value. So, the optimal influence of incubation time for HCH nanoflowers was selected to be 90 min for subsequent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of colorimetric immunoassay for E. coli O157:H7 detection
ce:section.ce:para: To study the feasibility of HCH nanoflowers based colorimetric immunoassay for E. coli O157:H7 detection, quantitative investigation of E. coli O157:H7 in PBS (pH 6.5) was performed under optimized conditions. Briefly, E. coli O157:H7 cells were incubated with HCH nanoflowers and polyclonal antibody modified MMs in PBS at 25 °C. After magnetic separation, the captured HCH nanoflowers were used for colorimetric analysis. Fig. 3 A shows that with the increase in the concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 from 101 to 106 CFU/mL, a gradually deepening tendency in the color of the immunosensing solution from light green to bright green was observed. The absorption peaks at 414 nm increased with the increasing E. coli O157:H7 concentration (Fig. 3B), and Fig. 3C presents the absorbance changes (A − A0) at 414 nm as the function of E. coli O157:H7 concentration. The linear range for E. coli O157:H7 detection was from 101 to 106 CFU/mL (R2 = 0.9926). The detection limit was estimated to be 4.1 CFU/mL (S/N = 3). As listed in Table S1, the fabricated colorimetric immunoassay based on HCH nanoflowers showed better performance for the detection of E. coli O157:H7. In future studies, the sensitivity of the proposed immunoassay can be combined with some HRP-mediated highly sensitive methods such as chemiluminescence, 33 plasmonic ELISA, 34 electrochemical immunosensor assay, and catalytic fluorescence. 35

Furthermore, the selectivity of the proposed method was examined using other bacterial samples, including S. aureus (Sta), L. monocytogenes (Lis), S. typhimurium (Sal), and E. coli O157:H7. The concentration of each pathogenic bacterial sample was 103 CFU/mL. As shown in Fig. 4 , the signals for the nontarget samples were much lower than those for the target of E. coli O157:H7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of E. coli O157:H7 in milk
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the analytical precision of the developed colorimetric immunosensing method for real complex biological samples, recovery experiments were used to determine whether the assay was affected by interfering factors. Different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 diluted in the milk sample and then analyzed for recovery. As shown in Table 1 , the analytical recoveries were in the range of 99−105.1%, which were within the acceptable criteria for bioanalytical method validation. The results demonstrated that the designed method provided a significant potential tool for practical application for the detection of E. coli O157:H7 in milk.

In conclusion, HCH nanoflowers were synthesized by a one-step co-precipitation method, and were found to catalyze oxidation of ABTS to generate a green product in the presence of H2O2. By combining HCH nanoflowers catalyzed chromogenic reaction with magnetic separation, HCH nanoflowers were developed as a novel enzyme mimics signal label and applied in colorimetric immunoassay method for the detection of E. coli O157:H7. Thus, the present study describes a new enzyme mimic with peroxidase-like activity and provides a potential method for colorimetric detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria food quality control.
We gratefully acknowledge support from Science and technology development plans of Jilin Province (20150101105JC) and The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2016YFD0501000).
The authors have declared that no competing interests exist.

ce:section.ce:label: A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bmcl.2018.07.017.
